name: Belchite, Codo and Almonacid
name_original: Codo y Almonacid de la Cuba — circular desde Belchite
description: This moderate 23.5-mile road cycling route from Belchite explores semi-desert landscapes, historical ruins, and the Roman Dam of Almonacid d
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Belchite, Spain, traverse a unique landscape characterized by arid terrain and extensive olive groves. The region's dry, open vistas provide a distinctive backdrop for cyclists, offering a sense of remoteness. Cyclists can explore vast plains, traditional agricultural fields, and steppe landscapes, which present diaphanous horizons and tranquil settings. Protected natural areas like El Planerón Reserve also offer opportunities to experience the diverse environment. Best road cycling routes around Belchite The most popular road cycling route is Gelsa and Pina de Ebro — circular from Quinto, an easy 29.5 miles (47.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 56 minutes to complete, passing through open plains and agricultural areas. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Lécera - Moneva - Moyuela - Azuara — circular from Letux, a moderate 38.1 miles (61.4 km) path. This route leads through rolling hills and arid landscapes, offering expansive views. Local road cyclists also love the Belchite, Codo and Almonacid, a 23.5 miles (37.8 km) trail leading through historical areas and arid plains, often completed in about 1 hour 39 minutes. Road cycling around Belchite is defined by desert sceneries, extensive olive groves, and steppe landscapes. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Belchite's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Belchite?
A: There are over 10 road cycling routes around Belchite, offering a variety of experiences. These include easy, moderate, and difficult options to suit different skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Belchite?
A: Road cycling around Belchite features a unique blend of arid, desert-like landscapes, vast plains with extensive olive groves, and tranquil steppe scenery. You'll encounter open vistas and a sense of remoteness, with routes passing through agricultural fields and rolling hills.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling routes in Belchite?
A: Yes, there are easy road cycling routes suitable for families. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992647804/gelsa-and-pina-de-ebro-circular-from-quinto" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gelsa and Pina de Ebro — circular from Quinto&lt;/a&gt; route is an easy 29.5-mile (47.5 km) trail that takes you through open plains and agricultural areas, perfect for a relaxed family ride.

Q: Can I bring my dog on road cycling routes in Belchite?
A: While many outdoor areas in Belchite are welcoming, specific regulations for dogs on cycling routes can vary. It's always best to ensure your dog is well-behaved and on a leash, especially in protected natural areas or near agricultural land. If using a trailer, ensure it's safe for your pet and doesn't obstruct other cyclists.

Q: What natural attractions or landmarks can I see while cycling?
A: You can explore several natural attractions. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3592739" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Poza de los Chorros&lt;/a&gt; offers a beautiful natural feature, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5335522" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Planerón Ornithological Reserve&lt;/a&gt; is accessible by bike and is a paradise for birdwatching, especially in spring. You might also encounter the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5146890" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salada de Mediana Salt Flat&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there historical sites to visit along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, some routes integrate historical areas. The poignant ruins of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5247438" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Village of Belchite&lt;/a&gt;, a reminder of the Spanish Civil War, can be passed by or near on some rides, adding a historical dimension to your journey. For more context on its history, you can visit &lt;a href="https://www.dark-tourism.com/index.php/704-belchite" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;dark-tourism.com&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Belchite?
A: Belchite's climate is characterized by dry, arid conditions. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for road cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, sometimes windy, conditions of winter. These seasons provide comfortable riding weather and allow you to fully appreciate the unique landscapes.

Q: What are the difficulty levels of road cycling routes in Belchite?
A: Belchite offers a range of difficulty levels. You'll find easy routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992647804/gelsa-and-pina-de-ebro-circular-from-quinto" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gelsa and Pina de Ebro — circular from Quinto&lt;/a&gt;, moderate options such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992591032/lecera-moneva-moyuela-azuara-circular-from-letux" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lécera - Moneva - Moyuela - Azuara — circular from Letux&lt;/a&gt;, and more challenging routes for experienced cyclists. In total, there are 2 easy, 6 moderate, and 4 difficult routes.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the Belchite area?
A: Yes, many routes in the Belchite area are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992647804/gelsa-and-pina-de-ebro-circular-from-quinto" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gelsa and Pina de Ebro — circular from Quinto&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992591032/lecera-moneva-moyuela-azuara-circular-from-letux" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lécera - Moneva - Moyuela - Azuara — circular from Letux&lt;/a&gt;, offering convenient loop options.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Belchite?
A: Cyclists often praise the unique, open vistas and the sense of remoteness offered by Belchite's arid and steppe landscapes. The extensive olive groves provide picturesque sections, and the variety of routes, from easy agricultural paths to more challenging climbs through rolling hills, caters to diverse preferences.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay in Belchite after my ride?
A: Belchite and its surrounding villages offer various amenities for visitors. You can find local cafes and restaurants to refuel after your ride, as well as accommodation options if you plan to stay longer. It's advisable to check availability, especially during peak seasons.

Q: Are there routes that pass by the Roman Dam of Almonacid de la Cuba?
A: Yes, some routes in the area will bring you close to or past the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5268320" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Viewpoint of the Roman Dam of Almonacid de la Cuba&lt;/a&gt;. This impressive historical structure is a notable landmark and offers scenic views, making it a great point of interest during your ride.
tips:
We are in the town of Belchite and we have the option of making a stop (mandatory if we have never been) to walk the streets of the Old Town of Belchite (with reservation) learning about the events that took place during the days of the Battle of Belchite. We cannot miss it if we want to know our history.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The Codo and Almonacid de la Cuba circular route is rated as moderate. It covers approximately 37.8 kilometers (23.5 miles) with an elevation gain of about 370 meters (1,214 feet). While there are no extreme climbs, the accumulated elevation requires good fitness. The route features mostly well-paved surfaces, making it suitable for road bikes, but be prepared for undulating terrain.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty, length, and elevation gain, this route is best suited for cyclists with at least a good level of fitness and some experience with road cycling. Beginners might find the sustained effort challenging, though the well-paved surfaces make it manageable for those looking to push themselves.

faqs.question: Where does the route start and are there parking facilities?
faqs.answer: The route is a circular loop starting from Belchite. You can typically find parking in or around the town of Belchite, which serves as a convenient starting and ending point for the loop. Specific parking facilities are usually available near the town center or main attractions.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and landmarks can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse and striking scenery. You'll cycle through a largely semi-desert landscape, beautifully contrasted by the oasis-like presence of the Aguasvivas river. Notable landmarks include the poignant ruins of the Old Village of Belchite, which was destroyed during the Spanish Civil War. In Almonacid de la Cuba, you can see its historical architecture, including a defensive Moorish tower, and the impressive Roman dam dating back to the 1st century. You'll also pass through the Olive Groves of Codo.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) when temperatures are milder and more pleasant for cycling. Summers can be very hot in the Aragon region, making midday cycling challenging. Winters are generally cooler but can be suitable on clear days.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route?
faqs.answer: No specific cycling permits are required to ride this route in the Campo de Belchite region of Aragon, Spain. There are also no general entrance fees for accessing the route itself. However, some specific historical sites, like the Old Village of Belchite, may have guided tour fees if you wish to enter the ruins.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is a public road cycling path, the suitability for dogs depends on their ability to keep up with a road bike for 37.8 km and the local regulations for dogs in towns and natural areas. If you plan to stop at attractions, check their specific dog policies. Generally, dogs are not recommended for accompanying cyclists on long road rides.

faqs.question: What are the road conditions and terrain like for cycling?
faqs.answer: The route is classified as a moderate road ride with mostly well-paved surfaces, making it suitable for road bikes. The region features a mix of plains and hills, so you can expect undulating terrain. Traffic levels are generally low, contributing to a pleasant cycling experience.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with several other paths. You will find yourself on sections of the Camino del Maestrazgo-Bajo Aragón, Almonacid de la Cuba-Belchite, Calle Mayor, Camino del Parque, Camino alto Lobo, Antigua Pista Cementerio, and the Ferrocarril Zaragoza-Utrillas.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for refreshments or water along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through towns like Codo and Almonacid de la Cuba, and starts from Belchite. These towns offer opportunities to find cafes, restaurants, or shops where you can purchase water and refreshments. It's always advisable to carry sufficient water, especially during warmer months, as services might be limited between towns.

faqs.question: What wildlife or natural features can I spot?
faqs.answer: The semi-desert landscape contrasted with the green corridor of the Aguasvivas river provides unique natural features. While specific wildlife sightings can vary, you might observe various bird species adapted to arid environments, and the flora will reflect the region's dry climate, with the exception of the lush areas around the river and the Olive Groves of Codo.
